extracted_characteristics.system_under_study: A Cyber-Physical System (CPS) and Physical Twin (PT) representing a thermal incubator system. The system consists of an insulated styrofoam box, a heatbed for heating, a fan for temperature distribution, two internal temperature sensors, one external temperature sensor, a Raspberry Pi controller, and a Printed Circuit Board (PCB). The operational domain involves maintaining a regulated temperature within the box regardless of internal content. Key characteristics include thermal inertia of the heatbed, dynamic temperature control, and integration with a Digital Twin for real-time monitoring and simulation.
extracted_characteristics.dt_services: 1. Real-time visualization of incubator status via dashboard using time series data from controller and sensor messages. 2. On-demand calibration using automated calibration processes (section 3.3) for model parameter adjustment. 3. Anomaly detection and heatbed state estimation via Kalman filter (section 4.2) using Model B. 4. What-if simulations through co-simulations with historical/real-time data from the Physical Twin. 5. Self-adaptation loop enabling dynamic reconfiguration when new objects (e.g., cold water) are introduced, closing the DT feedback loop.
extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers: 1. Hardware: Raspberry Pi controller, styrofoam insulated container, heatbed, fan, temperature sensors (T2/T3 at 18V), PCB, power supply. 2. Software: Open-source simulation models (Model A/B with 2/4 free parameters), time series database for data logging, Kalman filter for state estimation, co-simulation frameworks for what-if scenarios. 3. Calibration tools: Parameter optimization for C_air, G_box, C_heater, G_heater (e.g., C_air=616.56 J/K for Model A, C_air=486.12 J/K for Model B). 4. Communication protocols: Controller Area Network (CAN) for car-like ECU communication (referenced in CPS examples). 5. Repository: Open-source hardware/software specifications available at https://github.com/INTO-CPS-Association/example-incubator.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_time_scale: Real-time visualization of incubator status is achieved through continuous data relay to a time series database, with Kalman filter-based anomaly detection and state estimation operating in real-time. Calibration processes (section 3.3) and co-simulations use historical/real-time data from the Physical Twin (PT) with temporal granularity tied to sensor sampling rates (e.g., temperature measurements). Model B demonstrates better prediction of heatbed inertia, implying finer temporal resolution for thermal dynamics.
extracted_characteristics.multiplicities: Two distinct models (Model A/B) with different parameter sets (2 vs 4 free parameters) are used. The system employs a decentralized architecture via RabbitMQ for message passing between controller, low-level driver, and Digital Twin components. Coordination occurs through shared data streams, with Model B's calibrated parameters (C_air, G_box, C_heater, G_heater) enabling specialized state estimation for heatbed dynamics.
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation: Orchestrated through RabbitMQ-based message brokering, integrating controller statecharts (Fig. 8), calibration algorithms (section 3.3), and co-simulations. The Digital Twin framework includes: 1) Calibration modules updating CPS models with parameter estimates, 2) Monitors checking safety/optimality using sensor data, 3) Self-adaptation loops triggering adjustments when new objects (e.g., cold water) are introduced. Resource management focuses on real-time data flow between PT and DT.
extracted_characteristics.horizontal_integration: Relies on RabbitMQ (https://www.rabbitmq.com/) for cross-component communication, enabling integration with external systems via message queues. The DT shares sensor data with co-simulations (similar to [17]) and uses open-source hardware/software (https://github.com/INTO-CPS-Association/example-incubator) for interoperability. No explicit mention of enterprise standards, but RabbitMQ's AMQP protocol enables protocol-level integration.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data: Digital Twin models include behavioral (differential equations for dynamics), analytical (heat equation Q=cmΔT), and simulation models (Model A/B with 2/4 free parameters). Data sources: sensors on Physical Twin (PT) for temperature, data management via time series databases, calibration algorithms updating models with parameter estimates. Models are validated via Kalman filters for state estimation and co-simulations for what-if scenarios. Model B (4 parameters) better predicts heatbed inertia, used for anomaly detection and real-time visualization.

Fan ensures uniform temperature distribution. Actuation limitations: heatbed power transfer efficiency (Assumption 4), fan air distribution. Remote control via controller and low-level drivers. Event triggers: Sensor data collection events, calibration algorithm updates. Data preprocessing: Calibration algorithms update models with parameter estimates. Data integration: RabbitMQ messages relay PT states to DT for simulation and monitoring.
extracted_characteristics.virtual_to_physical_interaction: Control flows from DT to PT include commands to control fan and heatbed via RabbitMQ. Command types: Fan control, heatbed activation. Message format: RabbitMQ messages with control signals. Validation: Safe protocol in low-level driver ensures commands stay within safe limits. Feedback: Controller receives state updates from PT via RabbitMQ. Conflict resolution: Not explicitly detailed. Command confirmation: Controller sends commands and waits for PT state updates to confirm execution.
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection: Technical network infrastructure uses RabbitMQ (AMQP) as the communication protocol. extracted_characteristics.dt_hosting_deployment: Deployment infrastructure uses Raspberry Pi as edge hosting platform. Computational requirements: Raspberry Pi handles control logic, data processing, and communication. Storage: Time series database for logging PT states. Scalability: Not explicitly detailed. Deployment models: Edge computing (Raspberry Pi) with potential cloud integration. Availability: Ensured via redundant data collection and safe protocol mechanisms.
extracted_characteristics.life_cycle_stages: The Digital Twin (DT) lifecycle includes development stages such as building simulation models (Model A and Model B), calibration processes, and integration into the Physical Twin (PT). The Incubator case study involves iterative development of models (e.g., Model A with 2 free parameters and Model B with 4 free parameters), calibration using energy flow equations (Q = cmΔT), and deployment of the DT framework for real-time monitoring and control. Transition management involves updating models with new data and integrating them into the DT system for continuous operation.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_process_and_dt_evolution: The twinning process involves building dynamic models of the Physical Twin (PT) with assumptions about thermal behavior, calibration using energy equations, and integration of models with monitoring and optimization algorithms. Version control is achieved through iterative model development (Model A vs. Model B). Evolution strategies include updating models with new data, using Kalman filters for state estimation, and co-simulations for what-if scenarios. The DT evolves through continuous calibration, anomaly detection, and self-adaptation loops triggered by environmental changes.
extracted_characteristics.insights_and_decision_making: The DT provides real-time visualization of the incubator status via dashboard data from the controller and sensors. Anomaly detection is achieved using Kalman filters with Model B for heatbed state estimation. What-if simulations enable optimization of controller parameters (e.g., fig. 8). Decision-making processes involve correlating measured data with non-measured heatbed temperatures and using simulation results to adjust control strategies. User interaction includes dashboards and data visualization tools for monitoring system performance.
extracted_characteristics.security_and_safety_considerations: The low-level driver incorporates a safe protocol to maintain device safety, including periodic temperature data reading and command validation through the communication server. Safety mechanisms involve preventing unsafe state transitions by ensuring temperature data is uploaded to the communication server before actuation. No explicit mention of cybersecurity protocols, access control, authentication systems, threat mitigation strategies, or compliance with safety-critical standards is provided.

### **2 Incubator Case Study**

## The Incubator Case Study for Digital Twin Engineering

Hao Feng _[†]_, Cl´audio Gomes _[†]_, Casper Thule _[†]_, Kenneth Lausdahl _[†]_,
Michael Sandberg _[†]_, and Peter Gorm Larsen _[†]_


_†_ DIGIT, Department of Electrical and Computer Engineering, Aarhus
University, Denmark


February 23, 2021


**Abstract**


To demystify the Digital Twin concept, we built a simple yet representative
thermal incubator system. The incubator is an insulated box fitted with a heatbed,
and complete with a software system for communication, a controller, and simulation models. We developed two simulation models to predict the temperature
inside the incubator, one with two free parameters and one with four free parameters. Our experiments showed that the latter model was better at predicting the
thermal inertia of the heatbed itself, which makes it more appropriate for further
development of the digital twin. The hardware and software used in this case study
are available open source, providing an accessible platform for those who want to
develop and verify their own techniques for digital twins.

### **1 Introduction**


Cyber-Physical Systems (CPSs) are integrations of computation with physical processes. Embedded computers act through a network, monitor and control the physical
processes [1].
The cyber part refers to the computation part and communications, while the physical part refers to the part of the system interacting with the physical world. The environment is the part that may significantly affect the properties of the CPS but cannot
directly be controlled.
An example of a CPS is a car. The physical part in the car contains a body, four
wheels, seats, an engine, and so on, which interact with the environment (e.g., wind,
road surface). The cyber part of the car is different control systems implemented in
Electronic Control Units (ECUs) that communicate among themselves through a Controller Area Network.

the goal of understanding one aspect of the system [2]. For example, a Computer-Aided
Design (CAD) model of a car may store information about the sizes of the wheels,
shapes of the chassis, styles of the car, and so on, but it does not contain information
about how the forces acting on the car affect its movement. An engineer who desires
to analyse the dynamics of the car may build a dynamic model described by the differential equations introduced in, e.g., [3, Section 5].
Beyond better understanding, models can assist with optimising the overall system,
discovering causes and effects, measuring consequences of changes, and communicating among engineers. Once the system is deployed, the models used to build it should
not be discarded. Instead, they should be integrated into a Digital Twin (DT).
DT is often defined as a framework that integrates modelling, simulation, monitoring, and optimisation technologies, with the intent of adding value to the use of the
Physical Twin (PT) [4,5]. For instance, a DT provides the ability to run simulations that
reproduce and predict the behaviour of the PT under its current operating conditions.
The following important parts are highlighted [6]:
**Data** – collected from the PT through sensors over time;
**Models** – knowledge about different aspects of both the cyber and the physics of the
PT and its environment [1] ; and
**Algorithms** – techniques that use data and models, manipulating those to generate
more data and knowledge (e.g., fault detectors, supervisory controllers, state estimators, optimisers).
An example common structure of a DT is represented in fig. 1. In fig. 1, the DT
employs calibration algorithms to update the models of the CPS and environment with
the best estimate of the parameters. The resulting data is then used to inform monitors
that check whether the system is performing safely and optimally. When violations are
detected, humans (or other algorithms) may decide to make changes to the physical
twin.

### **2 Incubator Case Study**


The main goal of the incubator is to reach a certain temperature within a box and
regulate it regardless of the content inside. The physical components in the incubator
form a plant that is controlled by a Raspberry Pi. The overall systematic diagram of
the Incubator is shown in fig. 2.


**2.1** **Hardware Setup**


We start with a brief description of the hardware components, back-of-the-envelope
calculations, and refer the reader to the online repository [3] for more details.
The plant of the Incubator consists of (see fig. 3):

  - A styrofoam box in order to have an insulated container.

  - A heatbed to heat up the content inside the styrofoam box.

  - A fan to distribute the heating inside the box.

  - Two temperature sensors to monitor the temperature inside the box.

  - One temperature sensor to monitor the temperature outside the box.

**Assumption 2** _The box walls do not accumulate heat, or such heat accumulation has_
_little effect on the air temperature inside the box._


**Assumption 3** _The specific heat capacity of the air inside the box is assumed constant,_
_even when pressure and humidity may change._


**Assumption 4** _The majority of electrical power is transferred to heat energy, which_

**2.2.2** **The Low-Level Driver**


The low-level driver is used to interact with the PT and to issue the states of the PT

to the RabbitMQ server. In order keep the device in a safe state, a safe protocol is
incorporated into the low-level driver.
The low level driver periodically reads the temperature data, and checks if there are
commands from the controller, through the communication server. The temperature
data is uploaded to the communication server.


**2.2.3** **The Controller**


The controller used in the case study is a comparable to a bang-bang controller, which
a small difference described below. The control strategy is executed after receiving the

temperature measurements.
The principle of bang-bang control is that when a reference temperature is given
to the controller, the controller issues a heating signal turning on the heatbed until the
measured temperature from sensors reaches the reference temperature. Because of the
delayed effect of the temperature, the controller needs to wait after each actuation, to
make sure the temperature does not rise too much. Figure 8 shows the state chart of the

shapes of the chassis, styles of the car, and so on, but it does not contain information
about how the forces acting on the car affect its movement. An engineer who desires
to analyse the dynamics of the car may build a dynamic model described by the differential equations introduced in, e.g., [3, Section 5].
Beyond better understanding, models can assist with optimising the overall system,
discovering causes and effects, measuring consequences of changes, and communicating among engineers. Once the system is deployed, the models used to build it should
not be discarded. Instead, they should be integrated into a Digital Twin (DT).
DT is often defined as a framework that integrates modelling, simulation, monitoring, and optimisation technologies, with the intent of adding value to the use of the
Physical Twin (PT) [4,5]. For instance, a DT provides the ability to run simulations that
reproduce and predict the behaviour of the PT under its current operating conditions.
The following important parts are highlighted [6]:
**Data** – collected from the PT through sensors over time;
**Models** – knowledge about different aspects of both the cyber and the physics of the
PT and its environment [1] ; and
**Algorithms** – techniques that use data and models, manipulating those to generate
more data and knowledge (e.g., fault detectors, supervisory controllers, state estimators, optimisers).
An example common structure of a DT is represented in fig. 1. In fig. 1, the DT
employs calibration algorithms to update the models of the CPS and environment with
the best estimate of the parameters. The resulting data is then used to inform monitors
that check whether the system is performing safely and optimally. When violations are
detected, humans (or other algorithms) may decide to make changes to the physical
twin.

data is uploaded to the communication server.

a small difference described below. The control strategy is executed after receiving the

temperature measurements.

to the controller, the controller issues a heating signal turning on the heatbed until the
measured temperature from sensors reaches the reference temperature. Because of the
delayed effect of the temperature, the controller needs to wait after each actuation, to
make sure the temperature does not rise too much. Figure 8 shows the state chart of the

  - Two temperature sensors to monitor the temperature inside the box.

Since systems are getting increasingly complex, models are needed to better comprehend their behaviour. A model is an abstract representation of a system, built with


1
block_4:
the goal of understanding one aspect of the system [2]. For example, a Computer-Aided
Design (CAD) model of a car may store information about the sizes of the wheels,
shapes of the chassis, styles of the car, and so on, but it does not contain information
about how the forces acting on the car affect its movement. An engineer who desires
to analyse the dynamics of the car may build a dynamic model described by the differential equations introduced in, e.g., [3, Section 5].
Beyond better understanding, models can assist with optimising the overall system,
discovering causes and effects, measuring consequences of changes, and communicating among engineers. Once the system is deployed, the models used to build it should
not be discarded. Instead, they should be integrated into a Digital Twin (DT).
DT is often defined as a framework that integrates modelling, simulation, monitoring, and optimisation technologies, with the intent of adding value to the use of the
Physical Twin (PT) [4,5]. For instance, a DT provides the ability to run simulations that
reproduce and predict the behaviour of the PT under its current operating conditions.
The following important parts are highlighted [6]:
**Data** – collected from the PT through sensors over time;
**Models** – knowledge about different aspects of both the cyber and the physics of the
PT and its environment [1] ; and
**Algorithms** – techniques that use data and models, manipulating those to generate
more data and knowledge (e.g., fault detectors, supervisory controllers, state estimators, optimisers).
An example common structure of a DT is represented in fig. 1. In fig. 1, the DT
employs calibration algorithms to update the models of the CPS and environment with
the best estimate of the parameters. The resulting data is then used to inform monitors
that check whether the system is performing safely and optimally. When violations are
detected, humans (or other algorithms) may decide to make changes to the physical
twin.

**2.2.2** **The Low-Level Driver**


The low-level driver is used to interact with the PT and to issue the states of the PT

to the RabbitMQ server. In order keep the device in a safe state, a safe protocol is
incorporated into the low-level driver.
The low level driver periodically reads the temperature data, and checks if there are
commands from the controller, through the communication server. The temperature
data is uploaded to the communication server.


**2.2.3** **The Controller**


The controller used in the case study is a comparable to a bang-bang controller, which
a small difference described below. The control strategy is executed after receiving the

temperature measurements.
The principle of bang-bang control is that when a reference temperature is given
to the controller, the controller issues a heating signal turning on the heatbed until the
measured temperature from sensors reaches the reference temperature. Because of the
delayed effect of the temperature, the controller needs to wait after each actuation, to
make sure the temperature does not rise too much. Figure 8 shows the state chart of the

  - A heatbed to heat up the content inside the styrofoam box.

  - Two temperature sensors to monitor the temperature inside the box.

The controller on the Raspberry Pi is listening from the RabbitMQ server for commands of controlling the fan and the heatbed and at the same time sharing the control
states to the RabbitMQ server.
We choose RabbitMQ because it greatly facilitates the digital twinning process, as
the DT can be implemented by components that listen to the messages being passed
between the controller and the load level driver, as illustrated in fig. 7.


**2.2.2** **The Low-Level Driver**


The low-level driver is used to interact with the PT and to issue the states of the PT

to the RabbitMQ server. In order keep the device in a safe state, a safe protocol is
incorporated into the low-level driver.
The low level driver periodically reads the temperature data, and checks if there are
commands from the controller, through the communication server. The temperature
data is uploaded to the communication server.


**2.2.3** **The Controller**


The controller used in the case study is a comparable to a bang-bang controller, which
a small difference described below. The control strategy is executed after receiving the

temperature measurements.
The principle of bang-bang control is that when a reference temperature is given
to the controller, the controller issues a heating signal turning on the heatbed until the
measured temperature from sensors reaches the reference temperature. Because of the
delayed effect of the temperature, the controller needs to wait after each actuation, to
make sure the temperature does not rise too much. Figure 8 shows the state chart of the
